era_context: Mid-17th century: The Thirty Years' War continues in Europe (ending 1648). The Ming Dynasty in China is in severe decline, facing both Manchu invasion and internal rebellion. The Ottoman Empire remains a major power. European colonial expansion accelerates in the Americas and Asia. Japan is consolidating the sakoku policy under Tokugawa Iemitsu.

events.title: Battle of Wittstock; Swedish victory in Thirty Years' War
events.region: Wittstock, Brandenburg, Holy Roman Empire (present-day Germany)
events.description: The Battle of Wittstock was fought between Swedish forces under Lennart Torstenson and Imperial forces in Brandenburg. The Swedish victory was a major success in the later stages of the Thirty Years' War, demonstrating Swedish military superiority and contributing to the eventual Swedish dominance in Central Europe. The battle was a turning point in the war's trajectory in northern Germany.

sources.name: Swedish military records and dispatches
Imperial records (Reichskanzlei)
Contemporary European chronicles

events.certainty_note: The battle is well-documented in Swedish and Imperial military records. The date and outcome are secure.
cross_references: Part of the Thirty Years' War (1618–1648)
Related to Swedish military dominance in the 1630s–1640s

events.title: Manchu forces invade Korea; Ming ally weakened
events.description: Manchu forces invaded Korea in 1636, forcing the Korean kingdom of Joseon to submit to Manchu overlordship. This invasion was part of the broader Manchu strategy to isolate Ming China and consolidate power in East Asia. The invasion weakened Ming's position by eliminating a potential ally and demonstrated Manchu military reach beyond Manchuria and Liaodong.

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing)
Korean historical records (Joseon Wangjo Sillok)
Ming court records

events.certainty_note: The Manchu invasion of Korea in 1636 is well-documented in both Qing and Korean records. The date is secure.
cross_references: Part of the Manchu conquest of East Asia
Related to fall of Ming Dynasty (1644)

events.title: Ming Dynasty faces internal collapse; Li Zicheng's rebellion grows
events.description: Li Zicheng's peasant rebellion, which had begun in the early 1630s, continued to grow in 1636, spreading across central and northern China. The rebellion exploited widespread famine, economic collapse, and Ming military weakness. The Ming government's inability to suppress the rebellion or address the underlying economic crisis contributed to the dynasty's terminal decline.

sources.name: Ming court records and military reports
Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing, later compilation)
Later Chinese historiography (e.g., Mingshi)

events.certainty_note: The broader context of Li Zicheng's rebellion is well-documented, but specific 1636 events are less precisely dated.
cross_references: Related to fall of Ming Dynasty (1644)

events.title: Manchu forces capture Yingzhou and other Ming fortifications
events.region: Liaodong Peninsula, Manchuria / Ming China border
events.description: Manchu forces under Hong Taiji continued their military campaigns against Ming-held territories in Liaodong, capturing strategic fortifications. These victories further weakened Ming control of the region and demonstrated the growing military superiority of the Manchus. The captures contributed to the Ming's inability to defend against both external Manchu pressure and internal rebellion.
key_figures: Ming commanders in Liaodong (names variable in sources)

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing)
Ming court records and military reports

events.certainty_note: The broader Manchu campaign context is well-documented, but specific 1636 captures are less precisely dated in surviving sources.
cross_references: Part of the Manchu conquest of Ming China (1618–1644)
Related to fall of Ming Dynasty (1644)

events.title: Mughal Empire under Shah Jahan; Taj Mahal under construction
events.description: The Taj Mahal, commissioned by Shah Jahan as a mausoleum for his deceased wife Mumtaz Mahal, was under construction in 1636, approximately 16 years into the project. The monument represented the pinnacle of Mughal architectural achievement and the empire's cultural and economic power. Shah Jahan's reign (1628–1658) was marked by extensive building projects and cultural patronage.

sources.name: Mughal court records and administrative documents
Contemporary Mughal historians (e.g., Abdul Hamid Lahori, Padshahnama)

events.certainty_note: The Taj Mahal's construction timeline is well-documented in Mughal court records.

events.title: Dutch East India Company (VOC) consolidates East Indies holdings
events.description: The Dutch East India Company (VOC) continued to consolidate its control over the spice trade and territorial holdings in the East Indies. The VOC's monopoly on spice production and trade was a major source of wealth for the Dutch Republic. The company's activities in 1636 included military campaigns against rival European traders and indigenous resistance.

sources.name: VOC records (Dutch East India Company archives)
Contemporary European accounts of the East Indies trade

events.certainty_note: The VOC's activities and consolidation are well-documented in company records.
cross_references: Part of the broader VOC expansion (1600s–1700s)
Related to European colonial expansion in Asia

events.title: English Civil War tensions escalate; Charles I's religious policies provoke opposition
events.description: Tensions between King Charles I and Parliament escalated in 1636, with Scottish resistance to Charles's religious policies intensifying. The king's attempt to impose Anglican church practices on Scotland provoked strong opposition, setting the stage for the First Bishops' War (1639). These tensions would eventually lead to the English Civil War (1642–1651).
key_figures: Charles I (King of England and Scotland)

sources.name: English and Scottish state papers
Contemporary English and Scottish chronicles

events.certainty_note: The escalation of tensions in 1636 is well-documented in English and Scottish state papers.

events.title: Ottoman Empire under Murad IV; internal consolidation
events.description: The Ottoman Empire, under Sultan Murad IV (r. 1623–1640), continued efforts to centralize authority and suppress internal dissent. Murad IV's reign was marked by military campaigns to restore Ottoman control over rebellious provinces and efforts to modernize the military. The empire remained a major military and political power in the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East.

sources.name: Ottoman court records and administrative documents
Contemporary Ottoman chronicles
European accounts of Ottoman affairs

events.certainty_note: The Ottoman Empire's status and Murad IV's reign are well-documented in Ottoman and European sources.
cross_references: Related to Ottoman history in the 17th century

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Wittstock's significance has been somewhat overstated in some Swedish historiography; while a major Swedish victory, it was one of several engagements in the broader Thirty Years' War. The characterization of Li Zicheng's rebellion as a unified movement in 1636 may be anachronistic; it was more a collection of bandit and peasant groups that gradually coalesced. The Manchu invasion of Korea in 1636 is sometimes dated to 1637 in older sources, but modern scholarship confirms 1636.
historiographic_note: The year 1636 is well-documented in European, East Asian, and South Asian sources. European records are particularly rich for the Thirty Years' War and English-Scottish political tensions. East Asian sources (Chinese, Japanese, Korean) are abundant for the Manchu invasion of Korea, Li Zicheng's rebellion, and Manchu-Ming conflict. Ottoman sources provide documentation of Murad IV's reign. However, the historical record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events. Economic and social conditions in most regions, and developments in sub-Saharan Africa and the Americas, are underrepresented. The narrative is shaped by the perspectives of the victors and the surviving literate classes.

graph_edges.note: Battle of Wittstock was part of the Thirty Years' War, which ended in 1648
Manchu invasion of Korea in 1636 weakened Ming's position and contributed to Ming Dynasty's collapse in 1644
Li Zicheng's rebellion's growth in 1636 contributed to Ming Dynasty's collapse in 1644
Manchu military campaigns in 1636 contributed to Ming Dynasty's collapse in 1644
Escalating tensions in 1636 led to First Bishops' War in 1639
